mnemonics.title: WC Options Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Remember WC options with phrase: 'Lions Wolves Cats Mice' = Lines Words Characters (or Bytes) Mice (for -m character count). The order matches default output: lines words bytes characters.

mnemonics.title: OD Options Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Remember OD options as: 'Aunt jennifer Takes No orders' = A(ddress) j(ump/skip) T(ype) N(limit). Also remember -v = 'v'iew all (no suppression of duplicates).

acronyms.usage: Linux command to count lines, words, bytes in files. Usage: wc -l file.txt shows line count.
Linux command to display file content in various formats. Used for binary file analysis. Usage: od -t x1 file.bin shows hex dump.
OD option to specify radix for file offsets. -A d=decimal, -A o=octal, -A x=hex, -A n=none.
OD option to specify output type/format. -t c=ASCII, -t x=hex, -t d=decimal, -t o=octal, -t f=float.

visualTips.visualization: Default wc output shows 4 numbers in order: [lines] [words] [bytes] [characters] filename. Think of it as a row of counters that fill up sequentially.
Each OD output line has two parts: [offset address] [16 bytes in chosen format]. Think of it as a ruler showing position and what's at that position.
The -t option format is -t [type][size]. Size is optional. Type letters: c=character, d=decimal, o=octal, x=hex, f=float. Size numbers: 1,2,4,8 bytes.

analogies.analogy: WC is like a receipt from a store that shows quantity of items - it gives you a quick summary/stats ticket about a file.
OD is like an X-ray or microscope for files - it reveals the internal raw structure and hidden bytes that normal text editors can't show.
OD -j is like fast-forwarding a video, OD -N is like setting a stop point - together they let you view specific segments of a file.
